data:
Text is edited to the left and shown as chart to the right (click to zoom) TextToTile is a very simple app that does one thing: it visualizes a text outline in a graphical way.
Even though the app itself is simple, it opens up a wide range of possibilities for gaining visual overviews of many types of structured data and texts. At the same time, it helps simplify your documents and gives you full control over where your data is stored and who can access it.
For some types of data, this may not feel critical—for example, when writing a shopping list or drafting an article. But when working on something like a book, or tracking personal biometrics in text files, it can be reassuring to know that only those with access to your computer (typically just you) can view and modify the data. No one else can share your data.
data.many-benefits#Many Benefits: This approach has many advantages. The app is generic—it is not designed for a specific type of data. An outline can represent the structure of anything you choose.
data.security#Security: Data security and privacy are top priorities in this app No data is sent to any server or backend No user information or activity is tracked Full anonymity—no accounts, no registration

data.editor#Editor:
Simple editor, no advanced features.
Indent using Tab and Shift+Tab. Pressing Enter keeps the current indentation level.
The editor can be positioned on the left or right side, as well as at the top or bottom of the app.
Text is edited to the left and shown as chart to the right (click to zoom) Below is the full text used in the example shown in the screenshot above.
Full text from screenshot above #gitte {@fill: green; } #susan {@fill: red;} #familie {@fill: blue; } #mom {@fill: #ff6b9a; @stroke: #b63b66; text: #88f;} #dad {@fill: #4d96ff; @stroke: #245bb5; text: #ffffff;} #lia {@fill: #9b5de5; @stroke: #5f2ca0; text: #ffffff;} #marcus {@fill: #00c2a8; @stroke: #00796b; text: #ffffff;} #weekend {@fill: #ccc; } #def {key: week; unit: week; min: 1; max: 52;} #def {key: energy; unit: score; min: 0; max: 100;} #def {key: stress; unit: score; min: 0; max: 100;} #def {key: mood; unit: score; min: 0; max: 10;} #def {key: load; unit: score; min: 0; max: 100;} #def {key: familyTime; unit: hours; min: 0; max: 8;} #def {key: duration; unit: minutes; min: 0; max: 300;} #def {key: effort; unit: score; min: 0; max: 10;} #def {key: focus; unit: score; min: 0; max: 10;} #def {key: confidence; unit: score; min: 0; max: 10;} #def {key: distance; unit: km; min: 10; max: 100;} #def {key: score; unit: points; min: 0; max: 50;} #def {key: cost; unit: currency; min: 0; max: 200;} #def {key: recovery; unit: score; min: 0; max: 10;} .top {--bold: 600; --xtext: black; } :root { --xfill: #f5faff; --xfill: #335553; } Works well with your regular editor
The built-in text editor is fine, but a natural use case is also to use the app as a viewer for outlines and files that you work on in other text-based applications, such as note apps like Obsidian or simple text editors like Visual Studio Code.
Auto-update when a file is edited—both in the built-in editor and in external apps.
Some apps save automatically (e.g. Obsidian), while others require an explicit save (e.g. Cmd/Ctrl+S).

data.styling-with-tags-and-levels#Styling wit:
Define general colors for tags that can be used on any line in the outline. Multiple tags can be used on a single line. The first tag determines the part’s grouping in the Tags view.
Define explicit colors for Section, Chapter, and Part (levels 1, 2, 3). For example, to highlight a specific day if the outline contains calendar data.

data.tags#Tags:
Overview using tags
Many people already use tags.
Tags = classes. The first tag on a part determines its grouping, but all tags are applied as CSS classes in the rendered visualization.

Structure view and Tags view. Select via the menu or with F1/F2.
The three levels can be used for anything: calendars, outlines, measurements, etc.
Zoom (and scale) available.

data.files-over-apps#Files over apps:
Edit data, not apps.
There is a growing trend — perhaps still a niche — toward focusing on your data as files that you can fully understand and keep track of.
Ideally in the simplest possible form: plain text.
Text files can still be read many years from now, even if the applications used to create them no longer exist.
The outlines you work with are simple text files, stored locally on your own computer.
data.markdown#Markdown:
Still just text files, but with a set of simple ways to style the content — covering much of what you would otherwise need a dedicated word processor for.
Examples include italic, bold, headings, tables, etc.
Regardless of which app you use to edit these files — such as the note-taking app Obsidian - the files and all the content you have created and refined remain fully readable, even if the app itself is no longer available.
data.single-source-of-truth#Single source of:
Edit your text and data in one place, while maintaining a visual overview — from the first idea to the final result, or as a dynamic view of ongoing updates and everything you need to keep track of.
I have tried many apps and platforms that were excellent in themselves, but often suited only specific, isolated phases of a project — such as brainstorming, planning, or reorganizing structure.
When I started writing the actual content — for example, a novel — and made changes there, it required a lot of effort to step out of the writing process just to keep a separate planning or visualization tool up to date. So much so that I eventually stopped maintaining those external tools in order to stay focused in writing mode.
Personally, I now use Obsidian for all types of writing — whether it’s quickly noting a phone number, drafting an email, or working on my next novel.
However, any text editor that saves content as plain text — rather than in a proprietary or unreadable format — can be used. Some apps, such as Obsidian, automatically save your work continuously, meaning the visual representation in TextToTile is always up to date without requiring manual saves. Other apps — such as Visual Studio Code or Notepad require explicit saving (Ctrl/Cmd+S) to ensure changes are reflected.
data.text-first-local-first#Text-first, local: Work with text as your primary format, and keep your data local.

data: Simple examples including the text source. The text source can be copy/pasted if you want to test it in your TextToEdit.

Here is a simple example…

- The Problem - Overwhelmed by ideas - Notes scattered across apps // #idea - Time is spent reorganizing instead of writing - Losing track of important thoughts // #problem - Constant rewriting of the same content // #process - Small changes force updates across multiple places - Structure changes break earlier drafts - Searching for structure - Trying traditional outlines // #process - Visual tools feel heavy and slow // #problem - Copying and pasting leads to inconsistencies - Need for a simpler approach // #insight - A different perspective - Writing first without worrying about structure // #idea - Letting patterns emerge naturally // #insight - Seeing connections between ideas // #result - The Discovery - A simple format - Text becomes the single source of truth // #concept - Indentation defines hierarchy // #concept - Tags add meaning and grouping // #concept - Visualizing structure - Turning text into a visual map // #process - Aligning related ideas across sections // #insight - Making flow easier to understand // #result - Iterating quickly - Moving blocks without friction // #process - Refining structure step by step // #process - Gaining confidence in the layout // #result - The Result - Clarity - Seeing the full document at a glance // #result - Understanding relationships between parts // #insight - Reducing mental overload // #result - Creativity - Focusing more on content than structure // #idea - Exploring ideas freely // #idea - Iterating faster with less effort // #process - Sharing the approach - Explaining the method to others // #concept - Getting positive feedback // #result - Turning the idea into a tool // #result

data: UI kontrol med Style. Eg. fill bg.

Who is the app for? The app was originally built for personal use (writing articles and novels) I had used other methods before, but was missing a tool that preserved a visual overview—even while restructuring, editing, and refining details During the process, I realized the app could be useful for others with similar needs—and possibly for creating structure and overview in areas I haven’t even considered yet Why was the app created? The app was created to solve a personal need: maintaining a clear visual overview while working with structured text Existing tools either focused on writing or planning, but rarely supported both in a simple and consistent way The goal was to combine simplicity, structure, and flexibility in one place Does the app use AI? There is no AI built into the app However, it is well suited for integration with AI tools (e.g. Claude Code or similar) AI can be used to read and maintain data in plain text files, which is much simpler than interacting with multiple complex graphical tools Many platforms for managing personal data are online systems, which you may not want to share your data with—and may not want AI systems to access or control No user accounts, no backend, no network calls What about security? Strong focus on security and privacy The app only has access to files and folders explicitly opened by the user No background access to your system or data What platform is the app built on? Built with Tauri (designed for strong security and minimal system access) The app has no network access and does not send or receive any data All graphics are rendered as SVG Text input (along with the toolbar and settings popup) is built using HTML Are there more views coming? Currently includes Structure view and Tags view Additional views are possible, such as a monthly calendar or other ways to visualize structured text Future development will likely be guided by practical needs—especially replacing existing tools where simple text-based management is sufficient How can I give feedback? Feedback and suggestions are very welcome Please share your ideas, use cases, or feature requests via GitHub Discussions or the project repository

data.styles-on-everything#Styles on everythin:
On Section, Chapter, Part
More tags = more classes.
Remember that only the first tag in a Part line is used for grouping the line in a tagblock
Simple Advanced

data.ai-collaboration-ai-agents#AI collaborat:
Access to files, not apps
For example, tools like Claude Code (and similar) can work directly with your text files. In everyday use, this could mean asking an AI to reorganize an outline, summarize sections, maintain structured data, or generate new content—directly in the files you already use.
I haven’t tested this extensively yet, but I plan to explore it and share my experiences.
This is conceptually separate from TextToTile’s role. The app’s purpose is simply to present a text-based structure/outline in a clear visual way. Who—or what—maintains the text (on your local computer) is not important to the app.
That said, TextToTile can be used to visualize dashboards, overviews, and plans that are partially or fully maintained by different AI agents, each handling specific tasks you define.

data.notes#Notes:
At the moment, the installation process is somewhat cumbersome. You may need to go through a few extra steps during installation.
This is because the app is still in beta and is currently gathering feedback.
